items.q: Is curating just hanging pictures neatly on a wall?
items.correct: No — curating involves selecting, sequencing, interpreting and contextualising works for an audience.
items.misconception: Yes — curators only arrange frames tidily.
items.why: Students reduce curating to decoration, missing its interpretive role in shaping how works are read together.

items.q: What is the idea behind the 'white cube' gallery space?
items.correct: Neutral white walls aim to isolate art from distraction — though critics argue the format itself carries ideology.
items.misconception: White walls are simply the cheapest paint option.
items.random_wrong: White keeps the artworks cold for preservation.
items.why: Students take the white cube as neutral or practical, missing O'Doherty's argument that it frames how we view art.

items.q: Why are works usually hung with their centres at a consistent height?
items.correct: A consistent centre line at average eye level creates a coherent viewing experience.
items.misconception: All frames must have their tops aligned instead.
items.random_wrong: It is required by fire regulations.
items.why: Students align the tops or bottoms of frames, producing jumpy sightlines instead of a steady centre line.

items.q: 'Inside the White Cube' was published in:
items.why: It's 1976. “1966” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Typical hang height (centre of work) is around:
items.why: Correct answer: 145-150 cm. “100 cm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a wall is 4.5 m long and works are hung 60 cm apart with no end-margin, how many works fit (counting both ends)?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
